title: Europe’s missile defence gap: is South Korea part of the answer?
author: IISS
text:
Lami Kim IISS Korea Chair in Advanced Technologies, National Security and DefenceAbout Lami

Online Analysis17th June 2026

Europe’s missile defence gap: is South Korea part of the answer?With the United States growing perceptibly unreliable as a defence provider, Europe faces a gap in its air and missile defence. South Korea is well positioned to fulfil some European defence needs.  

While demand for air- and missile-defence capability in Europe has increased significantly in recent years, the supply of such systems has failed to keep pace. The United States, on which Europe has long relied for advanced missile-defence systems, faces growing constraints in both its capacity and, potentially, its willingness to meet Europe’s requirements. Europe’s capability gap could be partially filled by South Korea.
Europe’s missile-defence gap
After decades of relative neglect following the Cold War, air and missile defence has returned to the top of Europe’s security agenda since Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Stockpiles have been markedly reduced as some Western and Central European countries – including Germany, the Netherlands, Romania and Spain – transferred US-supplied Raytheon Patriot systems to Ukraine following the outbreak of war. At the same time, many Eastern European countries have sought to phase out legacy Soviet-era systems, further increasing demand. Against this backdrop, air and missile defence was among the seven critical capability areas identified as requiring urgent action to close existing gaps in the 2025 White Paper for European Defence – Readiness 2030. To meet urgent needs, European governments have spent heavily – reportedly around US$50 billion since 2022 – primarily on US systems.
However, the expectation that the US will continue to meet all of Europe’s needs may prove increasingly unrealistic. With Washington’s long-term strategic focus fixed on the Indo-Pacific and its attention currently absorbed by the Middle East, Europe has become, at best, a tertiary security interest. President Donald Trump’s long-standing scepticism towards NATO, combined with recent tensions over European responses to US military operations against Iran, has served only to further seed doubt in Europe as to whether Washington remains a reliable security guarantor. Recent reports of US plans to withdraw approximately 5,000 troops from Germany and the potential cancellation of a 2024 agreement to deploy a multidomain task force to Germany, including long-range missiles, add to such concern.
Even without political friction, Washington’s ability to supply Europe is increasingly constrained. Parts of the US defence-industrial base were already under strain due to the United States’ support for Ukraine’s and Israel’s war efforts. Subsequent US military operations against Iran further reduced critical missile inventories. Up to 20% of Washington’s SM-3 interceptors and 20–50% of Lockheed Martin Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) missiles were expended by the US in the June 2025 conflict involving Iran and Israel. Operation Epic Fury, launched in February 2026, further reduced both US and Middle Eastern stocks. According to one estimate, Gulf countries expended approximately 800 Patriot missile interceptors. To put this into perspective, Lockheed Martin produced 620 Patriot missile interceptors in 2025. The resulting depletion in inventory holdings prompted the US to redeploy Patriot systems from South Korea to the Middle East in early 2026.
Under such conditions, the US is likely to prioritise replenishing its own stocks before fulfilling external demand. When exports do resume, its Gulf partners may take precedence. Europe could be near the end of the queue.
The resulting capability shortfall of Europe is acute in medium- and long-range ballistic-missile defence. Europe has relied mainly on the US-made Patriot system. Germany has purchased the Arrow 3 long-range air-defence system, developed by Israel Aerospace Industries with Boeing as a co-developer and co-producer, but this represents only a small fraction of the air- and missile- defence capacity Europe needs. The National Advanced Surface-to-Air Missile System (NASAMS), jointly developed by Norway and the US, is effective against cruise missiles, aircraft and many uninhabited aerial systems but not ballistic missiles. Likewise, Germany’s IRIS-T SLM system is optimised for air defence against cruise missiles and uninhabited systems rather than high-end ballistic threats. Europe has an alternative to Patriot – the SAMP/T surface-to-air missile system, developed by the Franco-Italian Eurosam consortium (MBDA and Thales). The system, however, has secured far fewer sales than its US competitor, and its production capacity remains constrained.
Can South Korea help?
Europe’s pressing need for air defence could present South Korea with further opportunities to secure sales on the continent. Although South Korea has not yet fully entered Europe’s missile-defence market, it has already demonstrated its ability to deliver major conventional systems quickly, supplying tanks, howitzers and aircraft to Poland while also securing contracts with Romania and Norway for K9 howitzers and K10 ammunition resupply vehicles, and with Norway and Estonia for Chunmoo multiple-launch rocket systems. According to a 2026 report by the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute, South Korea was the second-largest supplier of major arms to European NATO members during the 2021–25 period, behind only the US.
South Korea’s Cheongung-II medium-range surface-to-air missile system, jointly produced by LIG Nex1, Hanwha Systems and Hanwha Aerospace, has had a positive debut in the Middle East. The United Arab Emirates (UAE) ordered the Cheongung-II in 2022. Two systems were deployed in early 2026, shortly before the outbreak of the Iran conflict, and reportedly achieved an interception success rate exceeding 96%, drawing significant international attention. The third one was delivered on an expedited, emergency basis. Additional orders from Saudi Arabia and Iraq, placed in 2024, remain pending.
One advantage of South Korea’s systems arguably lies in their high degree of domestic production – estimated at around 95% – which reduces vulnerability to supply-chain disruptions and export restrictions while helping to keep production costs low.
Looking ahead, South Korea’s L-SAM system – sometimes described as a Korean equivalent to THAAD – could further enhance its appeal as a supplier of higher-tier missile-defence capabilities once the system is fully ready for deployment, expected by 2028.
Challenges, however, remain. One concern is whether South Korean defence firms can meet additional demand, which could potentially stretch production capacity. That said, current factory utilisation rates remain below capacity, and LIG Next1 is expanding its domestic production facilities. In addition, Hanwha is expanding production in Europe, including building new facilities in Poland and Romania. Although these plants are not intended for missile-interceptor production, they could potentially be adapted for that purpose, thereby significantly enhancing output and reducing delivery timelines for European customers.
A more fundamental obstacle lies in Europe’s industrial policy aimed at revitalising its own defence-industrial base. The European Union’s Security Action for Europe (SAFE) initiative, adopted in May 2025, allocates €150bn to strengthen the continent’s defence-industrial base. To qualify for a loan under this initiative, projects must meet a requirement that at least 65% of the product is produced within the EU, effectively prioritising domestic production. South Korea’s exclusion from SAFE limits the attractiveness of its systems for EU-funded procurement.
Striking a balance
Europe faces a dilemma. On the one hand, it must rebuild and strengthen its own defence-industrial base to ensure long-term autonomy. On the other hand, it must address immediate capability gaps in missile defence. These objectives are not mutually exclusive, but they require balancing.
While Europe has demonstrated strong political will and committed substantial financial resources to enhancing its capabilities, these efforts do not automatically translate into rapid increases in industrial output. In the near term, external partners such as South Korea would be well placed to help Europe enhance readiness and reduce reliance on the US.

AuthorLami Kim IISS Korea Chair in Advanced Technologies, National Security and Defence
